summary: DHH argues that reading habits should be private, drawing a parallel between the FBI's 1973 Library Awareness Program and today's Big Tech surveillance of what people read, follow, and subscribe to. He notes that Americans once deeply valued the principle that reading habits are nobody's business, yet now Twitter followers, Amazon purchases, and social media engagement are publicly visible and increasingly used to judge people. He describes how retweeting writers like Glenn Greenwald or Matt Taibbi now invites social condemnation, creating a chilling effect on intellectual curiosity. He calls for custodians of reading data — from librarians to social media platforms — to treat reading habits as private by default.
key_points: The FBI's Library Awareness Program once scandalized Americans, yet today Big Tech tracks reading habits far more invasively with little outcry
Social media platforms like Twitter make following lists public, turning reading choices into acts of social transgression subject to mob judgment
The acceleration of cancel culture creates expanding 'rings of incrimination' where not just writing but reading subversive content becomes punishable
Email newsletters (outside Gmail) remain one of the few private reading channels, which DHH holds up as the correct model
DHH acknowledges the complexity of content moderation questions while arguing the chilling effect on reading diversity is a serious problem
money_quotes: What you read is none of anyone's business.
We're well into the phase where the rings of incrimination are spreading. It's bad to be the original author of subversive writings, but it's increasingly bad to be a reader as well.

How bizarre it would have seemed back then to learn that in the future your reading habits would not only be tracked forever by Big Tech, and thus available to any agent with a subpoena, but broadcast to the world in a variety of uncomfortable ways.

It's bizarre you can't easily make your reading habits private on Twitter. You either have to hack the system with private lists, turn your account private, or keep a separate burner account with your subversive reading list. That's backwards.
But it has given me a renewed vigor to seek out subversive, alternative voices. To broaden my reading habits further, rather than shrink them down.

We need to bring back the idea that custodians of your reading habits, be they librarians or social media operators or online book sellers, should take a professional oath to keep your reading habits private.
These are big, difficult questions. And it's clear that we don't have big, easy answers.
key_insight: The principle that reading habits should be private — once so strongly held it triggered congressional hearings — has been quietly abandoned in the age of Big Tech surveillance and social media mob judgment, and we need to reclaim it.
